2160.text: (2) the reference number of the claim;

2161.text: (3) the full name of the applicant;

2162.text: (4) where the applicant is not already a party, his address for service ; and

2163.text: (5) either a request for a hearing or a request that the application be dealt with without a hearing.

2165.text: On receipt of an application notice containing a request for a hearing the Court will notify the applicant of the time and date for the hearing of the application.

2166.text: Service Of A Copy Of An Application Notice

2168.text: Unless the Court otherwise directs, or Rule 23.5 applies a copy of the application notice :

2169.text:
(1) must be served as soon as practicable after it is filed; and   
(2) except where another time limit is specified in these Rules , must in any event be served at least 3 days before the Court is to deal with the application.

2171.text: When a copy of an application notice is served it must be accompanied by:

2172.text: (1) a copy of any written evidence in support; and (2) a copy of any draft order which the applicant has attached to his application.

2175.text: (1) an application notice is served; but

2176.text: (2) the period of notice is shorter than the period required by these Rules ;

2177.text: the Court may direct that, in the circumstances of the case, sufficient notice has been given and hear the application.

2178.text: II EVIDENCE IN SUPPORT OF APPLICATIONS

2181.text: Rule 23.25(1) does not require written evidence:

2182.text: (1) to be filed if it has already been filed; or

2183.text: (2) to be served on a party on whom it has already been served.

2186.text: The requirement for evidence in certain types of applications is set out in some of the Rules . Where there is no specific requirement to provide evidence it should be borne in mind that, as a practical matter, the Court will often need to be satisfied by evidence of the facts that are relied on in support of or for opposing the application.

2188.text: The Court may give directions for the filing of evidence in support of or opposing a particular application. The Court may also give directions for the filing of evidence in relation to any hearing that it fixes on its own initiative. The directions may specify the form that evidence is to take and when it is to be served.

2190.text: Where it is intended to rely on evidence which is not contained in the application itself, the evidence, if it has not already been served, should be served with the application.

2192.text: Where a respondent to an application wishes to rely on evidence which has not yet been served, he should serve it as soon as possible and in any event in accordance with any directions the Court may have given.

2194.text: If it is necessary for the applicant to serve any evidence in reply, it should be served as soon as possible and in any event in accordance with any directions the Court may have given.

2196.text: Evidence must be filed with the Court as well as served on the parties. Exhibits should not be filed unless the Court otherwise directs.

2197.text: Evidence Verified By Statement Of Truth

2199.text: Although evidence may be given by affidavit , the Court will generally exercise its discretion under Article 51(1) of the Court Law to permit evidence to be given by witness statement, except where Part 29 requires evidence to be given on affidavit (as, for example, in the case of an application for a freezing order or a search order). In other cases the Court may order that evidence be given by affidavit .

2201.text: Witness statements and affidavits must comply with the requirements of Part 29, save that photocopy documents should be used unless the Court orders otherwise.

2203.text: Witness statements must be verified by a statement of truth signed by the maker of the statement.

2205.text: At hearings other than trial, an applicant may rely on the application notice itself, and a party may rely on his statement of case , if the application notice or statement of case (as the case may be) is verified by a statement of truth.

2207.text: Proceedings for contempt of court may be brought against a person who makes, or causes to be made, a false statement in a witness statement (or any other document verified by a statement of truth) without an honest belief in its truth. Evidence in support of an application for contempt of court should generally be given by affidavit unless the Court orders otherwise.

2211.text: Applications likely to require an oral hearing lasting 2 hours or less are regarded as “ordinary” applications.

2213.text: Many ordinary applications are very short indeed (e.g. applications to extend time). Such applications can normally be heard without evidence and on short (i.e. a few days) notice.

2215.text: Where evidence is necessary, the timetable for ordinary applications is as follows:

2216.text: (1) evidence in support must be filed and served with the application;

2217.text: (2) evidence in answer must be filed and served within 14 days thereafter;

2218.text: (3) evidence in reply (if any) must be filed and served within 7 days thereafter.

2220.text: The timetable may be abridged or extended by agreement between the parties provided that any date fixed for the hearing of the application is not affected. In appropriate cases, this timetable may be abridged by the Court .

2222.text: If the date fixed for the hearing of an application means that the times in Rules 23.41(2) and 23.41(3) cannot both be achieved, the evidence must be filed and served:

2223.text: (1) as soon as possible; and (2) in sufficient time to ensure that the application may fairly proceed on the date fixed.

2226.text: Applications likely to require an oral hearing lasting more than 2 hours are regarded as “heavy” applications.

2228.text: Heavy applications normally involve a greater volume of evidence and other documents and more extensive issues. They accordingly require a longer leadtime for preparation and exchange of evidence.

2230.text: The timetable for heavy applications is as follows:

2233.text: The timetable may be abridged or extended by agreement between the parties provided that any date fixed for the hearing of the application is not affected. In appropriate cases, the timetable may be abridged by the Court.

2236.text: In all cases the parties must liaise with the Registrar :

2237.text: (1) for directions as to the preparations for the hearing of the application; (2) to fix a date for the hearing; and (3) for directions as to the management of the hearing.

2238.text: Hearing Dates, Time Estimates And Time Limits

2240.text: Not later than 5 days before the date fixed for the hearing the applicant must provide the Registry with his current estimate of the time required to dispose of the application.

2242.text: It is essential for the efficient conduct of the Court’s business that the parties inform the Court of the reading required in order to enable the Judge to dispose of the application within the time allowed for the hearing and of the time likely to be required for that purpose. Accordingly:

2243.text: (1) each party must lodge with the Registry a reading list with an estimate of the time required to complete the reading; and

2244.text: (2) each party’s reading list should identify the material on both sides which the Court needs to read.

2246.text: All time estimates should be given on the assumption that the Judge will have read in advance the skeleton arguments and the documents identified in the reading list lodged in accordance with Rule 23.50(1).

2248.text: If at any time either party considers that there is a material risk that the hearing of the application will exceed the time currently allowed, it must inform the Registry immediately.

2250.text: A time estimate for an ordinary application should allow time for judgment and consequential matters; a time estimate for a heavy application should not.

2252.text: Save in the situation referred to in Rule 23.55, a separate estimate must be given for each application, including any application issued after, but to be heard at the same time as, another application.

2254.text: A separate estimate need not be given for any application issued after, but to be heard at the same time as, another application where the legal representative in the case certifies in writing that:

2255.text: (1) the determination of the application first issued will necessarily determine the application issued subsequently; or

2256.text: (2) the matters raised in the application issued subsequently are not contested.

2258.text: If it is found at the hearing that the time required for the hearing has been significantly underestimated,

2259.text: the Judge hearing the application may adjourn the matter and may make any special costs orders (including orders for the immediate payment of costs) as may be appropriate.

2263.text: Bundles for use on applications may be compiled in any convenient manner but must contain the following documents (preferably in separate sections in the following order):

2266.text: Copies of the statements of case and of previous orders in the action should be provided in a separate section of the bundle. They should not be exhibited to witness statements.

2268.text: Witness statements and affidavits previously filed in the same proceedings should be included in the bundle at a convenient location. They should not be exhibited to witness statements.

2270.text: The applicant must, as a matter of course, provide all other parties to the application with a copy of the application bundle at the cost of the receiving party. Further copies should be supplied on request, again at the cost of the receiving party.

2273.text: On some applications there will be key legal authorities that it would be useful for the Judge to read before the oral hearing of the application. Copies of these authorities should be provided with the skeleton arguments.

2275.text: It is also desirable for bundles of all of the legal authorities on which the parties wish to rely to be provided to the Judge hearing the application as soon as possible after skeleton arguments have been exchanged.

2278.text: Skeleton arguments (with a chronology unless one is unnecessary, and with a list of persons if one is warranted) must be lodged with the Registry and served on the legal representativesfor all other parties to the application. Guidelines on the preparation of chronologies are set out in Schedule A to this Part.

2281.text: (1) Where the hearing is fixed for a Sunday, or for a Monday where the Judge is hearing other matters on the Sunday, unless the Court orders otherwise:

2282.text: (a) All bundles to be submitted for the hearing of the Heavy Application must be received by the DIFC Courts Registry by 10am on the Wednesday prior to the hearing date.

2283.text: (b) Skeleton arguments shall be exchanged and filed with the DIFC Courts by no later than 2pm on the Thursday prior to the hearing.

2284.text: (2) For all other applications or those to be heard by video conference, all bundles must be received by the DIFC Courts Registry by

2285.text: (a) 10am on the Wednesday prior to any hearing fixed for a Monday or Tuesday, with Skeleton arguments to be filed with the Courts by 2pm on the Thursday prior to the hearing and

2286.text: (b) 10am on the Sunday prior to any hearing fixed for a Wednesday or Thursday with Skeleton arguments to be filed with the Courts by 2pm on the day falling two working days prior to the hearing.”

2288.text: For each day that a submission or filing for a hearing as set out in 23.4 above is delayed, a charge of $\cup 5 \$ 200$ will be applied to the party responsible for making the filing.

2290.text: Problems with the lodging of bundles or skeleton arguments should be notified to the Registrar as far in advance as possible. If the application bundle or skeleton argument is not lodged by the time specified, the application may be stood out of the list without further warning.

2292.text: Failure to comply with the requirements for lodging bundles for the application will normally result in the application not being heard on the date fixed at the expense of the party in default. An order for immediate payment of costs may be made.

2294.text: The parties must anticipate that at any hearing the Court may wish to review the conduct of the case as a

2295.text: whole and give any necessary case management directions. They should be ready to assist the Court in doing so and to answer questions the Court may ask for this purpose.

2297.text: Applications Which May Be Dealt With Without A Hearing

2299.text: The Court may deal with an application without a hearing if:

2300.text: (1) the parties agree as to the terms of the order sought; (2) the parties agree that the Court should dispose of the application without a hearing; or (3) the Court does not consider that a hearing would be appropriate.

2303.text: Rule 36.28 sets out the circumstances where an agreed judgment or order may be entered and sealed.

2305.text: Where an agreed order is to be made by a Judge the order must be drawn so that the Judge’s name and judicial title can be inserted.

2307.text: The parties to an application for an agreed order must ensure that they provide the Court with any material it needs to be satisfied that it is appropriate to make the order. Subject to any Rule or Practice Direction, a letter will generally be acceptable for this purpose.

2309.text: Agreed orders may be submitted to the Court in draft for approval and initialling without the need for attendance.

2310.text: (1) Two copies of the draft, one of which (or a counterpart) must be signed on behalf of all parties to whom it relates, should be lodged at the Registry . The copies should be undated. The order will be dated with the date on which the Judge initials it, but that does not prevent the parties acting on their agreement immediately if they wish.

2311.text: (2) The parties should act promptly in lodging the copies at the Registry . If it is important that the orders are made by a particular date, that fact (and the reasons for it) should be notified in writing to the Registry .

2313.text: Where a judgment or order has been agreed in respect of an application or claim where a hearing date has

2314.text: been fixed, the parties must inform the Court immediately.

2317.text: If the applicant considers that the application is suitable for determination on paper, he should ensure before lodging the papers with the Court:

2318.text: (1) that the application notice together with any supporting evidence has been served on the respondent ;

2319.text: (2) that the respondent has been allowed the appropriate period of time in which to serve evidence in opposition;

2320.text: (3) that any evidence in reply has been served on the respondent ; and

2321.text: (4) that there is included in the papers

2322.text: (a) the written consent of the respondent to the disposal of the application without a hearing; or

2323.text: (b) a statement by the applicant of the grounds on which he seeks to have the application disposed of without a hearing, together with confirmation that a copy has been served on the respondent .

2325.text: The Court at its discretion may dispose of an application without a hearing in the absence of the respondent’s consent.

2327.text: Certain applications relating to the management of proceedings may conveniently be made in correspondence without issuing an application notice . It must be clearly understood that such applications are not applications without notice and the applicant must therefore ensure that a copy of the letter making the application is sent to all other parties to the proceedings. Accordingly, the following procedure should be followed when making an application of this kind:

2328.text: (1) the applicant should first ascertain whether the application is opposed by the other parties;

2329.text: (2) if it is, the applicant should apply to the Court by letter stating the nature of the order which it seeks and the grounds on which the application is made;

2330.text: (3) a copy of the letter should be sent (by fax or email where possible) to all other parties at the same time as it is sent to the Court ;

2331.text: (4) any other party wishing to make representations should do so by letter within two days (i.e. two clear days) of the date of the applicant’s letter of application. The representations should be sent (by fax or email where possible) to the applicant and all other parties at the same time as they are sent to the Court ; and

2332.text: (5) the Court will advise its decision by letter to the applicant. The applicant must forthwith copy the

2333.text: Court’s letter to all other parties, by email where possible.

2334.text: Court Does Not Consider Hearing Appropriate

2336.text: Where Rule 23.69(3) applies the Court will treat the application as if it were proposing to make an order on its own initiative.

2339.text: Applications (other than arbitration applications) will be heard in public in accordance with Article 13 of the Court Law , save where otherwise ordered.

2341.text: With certain exceptions, arbitration applications will normally be heard in private.

2343.text: An application without notice for a freezing order or a search order will normally be heard in private.

2344.text: Hearings By Video Link, Telephone, Electronic Device Or Other Appropriate Means

2346.text: Parties must consider whether hearings can conveniently take place by video link, telephone, electronic device or other appropriate means in accordance with Article 51 of the Court Law .

2348.text: (1) The general rule for all hearings before the DIFC Courts is that the advocate for each party should appear physically in the DIFC Courts

2349.text: (2) There may be exceptional situations in which an advocate is unable to appear, for example due to the urgency of the matter, or because the estimated cost of his appearance in person before the Court is wholly disproportionate to the matters to be addressed at the hearing. In such circumstances that advocate may, by application on notice under RDC Part 23.77, request permission from the Registrar to participate in a hearing by video or telephone conference.

2351.text: Guidelines on the conduct of hearings by video link and telephone are set out Schedules B and C to this Part.

2352.text: Power Of The Court To Proceed In The Absence Of A Party

2354.text: Where the applicant or any respondent fails to attend the hearing of an application, the Court may proceed in his absence.

2357.text: (1) the applicant or any respondent fails to attend the hearing of an application; and

2358.text: (2) the Court makes an order at the hearing;

2359.text: the Court may, on application or of its own initiative, re-list the application.

2361.text: The power to re-list the application in Rule 23.86 is in addition to any other powers of the Court with regard to the order (for example to set aside , vary, discharge or suspend the order).

2364.text: The Court will keep, either by way of a note or a tape recording, brief details of all proceedings before it, including the dates of the proceedings and a short statement of the decision taken at each hearing.

2365.text: Schedule C to Part 23 Telephone hearings-1

2367.text: Orders Generally Drawn Up By The Parties

2369.text: Except for orders made by the Court on its own initiative and unless the Court orders otherwise, every judgment , order or direction will be drawn up by the parties.

2372.text: An application for an agreed order must include a draft of the proposed order signed on behalf of all the parties to whom it relates in accordance with Rule 23.73(1).

2375.text: Parties are reminded of the obligation to produce Arabic translations of any judgment , order or direction in accordance with Rule 2.4.

2378.text: Where an order provides a time by which something is to be done, the order should wherever possible state the particular date by which the thing is to be done rather than specify a period of time from a particular date or event.

2379.text: Service Of Application Where Application Made Without Notice

2381.text: This Rule applies where the Court has disposed of an application which it permitted to be made without service of a copy of the application notice .

2382.text: (1) Where the Court makes an order, whether granting or dismissing the application, a copy of the application notice and any evidence in support must, unless the Court orders otherwise, be served with the order on any party or other person:

2383.text: (a) against whom the order was made; and

2384.text: (b) against whom the order was sought.

2385.text: (2) The order must contain a statement of the right to make an application to set aside or vary the order under Rule 23.94.

2387.text: A person who was not served with a copy of the application notice before an order was made under Rule 23.91, may apply to have the order set aside or varied.

2389.text: An application under this Rule 23.94 must be made within 7 days after the date on which the order was served on the person making the application.

2393.text: Attention is drawn to Part 38 and, in particular, to the Court’s power to make an immediate assessment of costs.

2395.text: Attention is also drawn to Rule 38.56 which provides that if an order makes no mention of costs, none are payable in respect of the proceedings to which it relates.

2397.text: In carrying out an immediate assessment of costs, the Court may have regard amongst other matters to:

2399.text: Schedule A to Part 23 Skeleton arguments, chronologies and indices

2400.text: Schedule B to Part 23 Video-conferencing protocol

2401.text: Schedule C to Part 23 Telephone hearings

2405.text: The Court may give immediate judgment against a claimant or defendant on the whole of a claim, part of a claim or on a particular issue if:

2406.text: (1) it considers that: (a) that claimant has no real prospect of succeeding on the claim or issue; or (b) that defendant has no real prospect of successfully defending the claim or issue; and (2) there is no other compelling reason why the case or issue should be disposed of at a trial.

2408.text: An application for immediate judgment under Rule 24.1 may be based on:

2409.text: (1) a point of law (including a question of construction of a document); (2) the evidence which can reasonably be expected to be available at trial or the lack of it; or

2412.text: Types Of Proceedings In Which Immediate Judgment Is Available

2413.text: 24.3 The Court may give immediate judgment against a claimant or defendant in any type of proceedings.

2416.text: A claimant may not apply for immediate judgment until the defendant against whom the application is made has filed:

2417.text: (1) an acknowledgement of service ; or (2) a defence; unless

2418.text: (a) the Court gives permission; or (b) a Practice Direction provides otherwise.

2420.text: If a claimant applies for immediate judgment before a defendant against whom the application is made has filed a defence, that defendant need not file a defence before the hearing.

2422.text: Where an immediate judgment hearing is fixed, the respondent (or the parties where the hearing is fixed of the Court’s own initiative) must be given at least 14 days’ notice of:

2423.text: (1) the date fixed for the hearing; and (2) the issues which it is proposed that the Court will decide at the hearing.

2426.text: The application notice must include a statement that it is an application for immediate judgment made under Part 24.

2428.text: The application notice or the evidence referred to in it or served with it must:

2429.text: (1) identify concisely any point of law or provision in a document on which the applicant relies; and/or

2430.text: (2) state that it is made because the applicant believes that on the evidence the respondent has no real prospect of succeeding on the claim or issue or (as the case may be) of successfully defending the claim or issue to which the application relates;

2431.text: and in either case state that the applicant knows of no other compelling reason why the case or issue should be disposed of at a trial.

2432.text: Evidence For The Purposes Of An Immediate Judgment Hearing

2434.text: The parties are to comply with Part 23 but it is not necessary for written evidence:

2435.text: (1) to be filed if it has already been filed; or (2) to be served on a party on whom it has already been served.

2437.text: Where an immediate judgment hearing is fixed by the Court of its own initiative:

2438.text: (1) any party who wishes to rely on written evidence at the hearing must:

2439.text:
(a) file the written evidence; and   
(b) unless the Court orders otherwise, serve copies on every other party to the proceedings; at least 7 days before the date of the hearing;

2440.text: (2) any party who wishes to rely on written evidence at the hearing in reply to any other party’s written evidence must:

2441.text: (a) file the written evidence in reply; and (b) unless the Court orders otherwise serve copies on every other party to the proceedings;

2442.text: at least 3 days before the date of the hearing.

2445.text: The orders the Court may make on an application under Part 24 include:

2446.text: (1) judgment on the claim or any part of the claim; (2) the striking out or dismissal of the claim; (3) the dismissal of the application; and (4) a conditional order.

2448.text: Where it appears to the Court possible that a claim or defence may succeed but improbable that it will do so, the Court may make a conditional order.

2450.text: A conditional order is an order which requires a party:

2451.text: (1) to pay a sum of money into Court ; or

2452.text: (2) to take a specified step in relation to his claim or defence, as the case may be, and provides that that party’s claim will be dismissed or his statement of case will be struck out if he does not comply.

2455.text: If a remedy sought by a claimant in his claim form includes, or necessarily involves, taking an account or making an inquiry, an application can be made under Part 24 by any party to the proceedings for an order directing any necessary accounts or inquiries to be taken or made.

2458.text: If a remedy sought by a claimant in his claim form includes a claim:

2459.text: (1) for specific performance of an agreement (whether in writing or not) for the sale, purchase, exchange, mortgage or charge of any property, or for the grant or assignment of a lease or tenancy of any property, with or without an alternative claim for damages ; or

2460.text: (2) for rescission of such an agreement; or

2461.text: (3) for the forfeiture or return of any deposit made under such an agreement;

2462.text: the claimant may apply under Part 24 for judgment .

2464.text: The claimant may do so at any time after the claim form has been served, whether or not the defendant has acknowledged service of the claim form, whether or not the time for acknowledging service has expired and whether or not any particulars of claim have been served.

2466.text: The application notice by which an application under Rule 24.15 is made must have attached to it the text of the order sought by the claimant .

2468.text: The application notice and a copy of every affidavit in support and of any exhibit referred to therein must be served on the defendant not less than 4 days before the hearing of the application.

2471.text: Where the Court dismisses the application or makes an order that does not completely dispose of the claim it may:

2472.text: (1) give directions as to the filing and service of a defence; and

2473.text: (2) give further directions about the management of the case.

2476.text: Attention is drawn to Part 38 and, in particular, to the Court’s power to make an immediate assessment of costs.

2478.text: Attention is also drawn to Rule 38.56 which provides that if an order makes no mention of costs, none are payable in respect of the proceedings to which it relates.

2479.text: Setting Aside Order For Immediate Judgment

2481.text: If an order for immediate judgment is made against a respondent who does not appear at the hearing of the application, the respondent may apply for the order to be set aside or varied (see also Rules 23.83 to 23.85).

2483.text: On the hearing of an application under Rule 24.22, the Court may make such order as it thinks just.

2484.text: PART 25 Interim Remedies And Security For Costs

2488.text: The Court may grant the following interim remedies:

2490.text: (4) an order authorising a person to enter any land or building in the possession of a party to the proceedings for the purposes of carrying out an order under sub-paragraph (3);

2491.text: (5) an order providing for the delivery up of any goods which are or may become the subject matter of subsequent proceedings in the Court or as to which any question may arise in proceedings, to the claimant or to a person appointed by the Court for the purpose on such terms and conditions as may be specified in the order;

2492.text: (6) an order (referred to as a ‘freezing order’):

2493.text: (a) restraining a party from removing from the jurisdiction assets located there; or (b) restraining a party from dealing with any assets whether located within the jurisdiction or not;

2494.text: (7) an order directing a party to provide information about the location of relevant property or assets or to provide information about relevant property or assets which are or may be the subject of an application for a freezing order;

2495.text: (8) an order (referred to as a ‘search order’) requiring a party to admit another party to premises for the purpose of preserving evidence;

2496.text: (9) an order for production of documents or inspection of property before a claim has been made;

2497.text: (10) an order for production of documents or inspection of property against a non-party;

2498.text: (11) an order (referred to as an order for interim payment) under Section II of this Part for payment by a defendant on account of any damages , debt or other sum (except costs) which the Court may hold the defendant liable to pay;

2499.text: (12) an order for a specified fund to be paid into Court or otherwise secured, where there is a dispute over a party’s right to the fund;

2500.text: (13) an order permitting a party seeking to recover personal property to pay money into Court pending the outcome of the proceedings and directing that, if he does so, the property shall be given up to him;

2501.text: (14) an order directing a party to prepare and file accounts relating to the dispute;

2502.text: (15) an order directing any account to be taken or inquiry to be made by the Court ;

2503.text: (16) an order appointing a receiver or receiver and manager, having such powers as the Court may see fit, of the property or any of the property of any body corporate;

2504.text: (17) where the person against whom the order is to be made is a natural person:

2505.text: (a) an order appointing a receiver or trustee, having such powers as the Court may see fit, of the property or any of the property of that person;

2506.text: (b) an order requiring that person to deliver up to the Court his passport and such other documents as the Court sees fit; or

2507.text: (c) an order prohibiting that person from leaving the DIFC , Dubai or the UAE without the consent of the Court ; and

2508.text: (18) any other remedy provided by any other law, whether enacted before or after these Rules .

2510.text: In Rules 25.1(3) and 25.1(7), relevant property means property (including land) which is the subject of a claim or as to which any question may arise on a claim.

2512.text: The Court may grant an interim remedy whether or not there has been a claim for a final remedy of that kind.

2514.text: 25.4 A Judge may make an order for any interim remedy.

2515.text: 25.5 The powers of the Registrar to make orders for interim remedies are set out in Rules 3.4 to 3.14.

2517.text: Time When An Order For An Interim Remedy May Be Made

2519.text: An order for an interim remedy may be made at any time, including:

2520.text: (1) before proceedings are started; and (2) after judgment has been given.

2523.text: (1) Rule 25.6 is subject to any Rule, Practice Direction or other enactment which provides otherwise;

2524.text: (2) the Court may grant an interim remedy before a claim has been made only if:

2525.text:
(a) the matter is urgent; or   
(b) it is otherwise desirable to do so in the interests of justice; and   
(c) unless the Court otherwise orders, a defendant may not apply for any of the orders listed in Rule 25.1 before he has filed either an acknowledgment of service or a defence.

2526.text: (3) Where the Court grants an interim remedy before a claim has been commenced, it may give directions requiring a claim to be commenced.

2527.text: (4) In particular, the Court need not direct that a claim be commenced where the application is made for production of documents or inspection of property before a claim is made.

2528.text: How To Apply For An Interim Remedy

2530.text: The Court may grant an interim remedy on an application made without notice if it appears to the Court that there are good reasons for not giving notice.

2532.text: An application for an interim remedy must be supported by evidence, unless the Court orders otherwise.

2534.text: If the applicant makes an application without giving notice, the evidence in support of the application must state the reasons why notice has not been given.

2535.text: Urgent Applications And Applications Without Notice

2537.text: Urgent applications and applications without notice fall into two categories:

2538.text: (1) applications where a claim form has already been issued; and

2539.text: (2) applications where a claim form has not yet been issued;

2540.text: and, in both cases, where notice of the application has not been given to the respondent.

2542.text: Where a party makes an urgent application:

2543.text: (1) The party should telephone the Court between 10:00am and 5:00pm Sunday to Thursday $0 \mathsf { n } + 9 1 7$ 4427 3333 and ask to be put in touch with the Registrar or Deputy Registrar. The party should state that the call relates to an urgent application and provide an estimate of when they will be ready to present the application;

2544.text: (2) Where the application is so urgent that it must be made outside these hours, the party should telephone the Court’s emergency telephone number on $^ +$ 971 4427 3331;

2545.text: (3) The applications will normally be dealt with at a court hearing but cases of extreme urgency may be dealt with by telephone;

2546.text: (4) At the hearing of the application, the Court may consist of a Judge and the Registrar sitting together;

2547.text: (5) When acting under the procedure set out in this Part, the Court shall set a Return Date for the hearing of the application before a Judge sitting alone, which shall not be later than three days from the date upon which the interim order is issued.

2549.text: Applications dealt with at a Court hearing after issue of a claim form:

2550.text: (1) the application notice , evidence in support and a draft order should be filed with the Court two hours before the hearing wherever possible;

2551.text: (2) if an application is made before the application notice has been issued, a draft order should be provided at the hearing, and the application notice and evidence in support must be filed with the Court on the same or next business day or as ordered by the Court ; and

2552.text: (3) except in cases where secrecy is essential, the applicant should take steps to notify the respondent informally of the application.

2554.text: Applications made before the issue of a claim form:

2555.text: (1) in addition to the provisions set out in Rule 25.13, unless the Court orders otherwise, either the applicant must undertake to the Court to issue a claim form immediately or the Court will give directions

2556.text: for the commencement of the claim;

2557.text: (2) where possible the claim form should be served with the order for the injunction ; and

2558.text: (3) an order made before the issue of a claim form should state in the title after the names of the applicant and respondent ‘the Claimant and Defendant in an Intended Action’.

2563.text: (3) the Judge will require a draft order to be emailed to him;

2564.text: (4) the application notice and evidence in support must be filed with the Court on the same or next working day or as ordered, together with two copies of the order for sealing;

2565.text: (5) injunctions will be heard by telephone only where the applicant is acting by legal representative .

2569.text: (1) the order sought; and (2) the date, time and place of the hearing.

2571.text: The application notice and evidence in support must be served as soon as practicable after issue and in any event not less than 3 days before the Court is due to hear the application.

2573.text: A draft of the order sought should be filed with the application notice .

2575.text: Unless the urgency means that this is not possible, the applicant should provide the Court at the earliest opportunity with a skeleton argument.

2578.text: Applications for search orders and freezing orders must be supported by affidavit evidence.
